query: mechanism of CD8+ T cell activation by viral peptides

retrieved_docs.title: Human T-Cell Lymphotropic Virus -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The interaction of the host immune response with HTLV-1-encoded genes has also been demonstrated to facilitate an environment of immunosuppression. Acute infection is noted to promote an IL-10-dominant cytokine profile, which is known to suppress the T cell response. CD8 T-cell exhaustion is secondary to persistent viral proliferation results in failure of virus-specific suppression: a mechanism believed to be important in both chronic viral-mediated immunosuppression and oncogenesis. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Active Immunity -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Cytotoxic T lymphocytes (CTL) develop from CD8 T cells in response to cytokines released from TH1 cells and play a role in destroying virally infected cells and tumor cells. CTLs bind to their target cells via antigen-presenting MHC I molecules. They destroy infected cells using perforin proteins, which boreholes in the membrane and allow an influx of granzymes into the cell to promote apoptosis. CTLs can also trigger apoptosis through FasL (on T cells) and Fas (target cells) protein binding. [4]

retrieved_docs.content: Total lymphocyte count T lymphocytes (CD3, CD4, and CD8) B lymphocytes (CD19 and CD20) CD4/CD8 ratio

retrieved_docs.title: Interferon -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Furthermore, interferons have significant immunomodulatory effects, with interferon-gamma being the predominant immunomodulatory interferon. Type 1 interferons act as antiviral cytokines. Alpha interferons activate NK cells, which in turn destroy viral and other targets; increase the proliferation of B cells, which secrete antibodies against pathogens; and enhance CD8 T cell response by upregulating the expression of MHC class 1 on surface cells. Type 1 interferons also upregulate IL-12Rb and increase the expression of MHC class II molecules. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Vaccine (Vaccination) (Archived) -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Vaccines contain one or various immunogens (peptides), which antigen-presenting cells can engulf, process, and present along with MCH antigens to CD4+ T cells. These lymphocytes can synthesize cytokines that activate humoral and cellular responses, including antibody production, activation of CD8+ T cells, macrophage stimulation, and other functions. [2] Memory cells can develop in this process. They can proliferate more quickly in further encounters with the antigen.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, T-Cell Lymphocyte -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: CD8+ T cells, also known as killer T cells (or cytotoxic T cells), are effector cells for cell-mediated immunity. Initially, CD8+ T cells are naive and must be activated to begin effector functions (i.e., immune functions). This activation occurs by interaction with pro-APCs (“professional” antigen-presenting cells), mainly dendritic cells in lymph nodules/follicles, and leads to an intracellular pathway that up-regulates more antigen-specific TCRs on the T cell and leads to effector functions. T cells can only recognize protein-based antigens. TCRs (T-cell receptor and their co-receptors, such as CD3 and CD8) found on these cells form a complex with the MHC 1 receptor and the antigen in question. Once an activated CD8+ migrates into circulation and finds its antigenic target (expressed on a virally infected cell or cell with intracellular bacteria, for example), it utilizes its killing function. [1] The killing function of CD8+ T cells is mediated by 1 of 2 mechanisms. The first mechanism involves the use of the Fas/Fas Ligand (FasL). Activated CD8+ T cells express FasL which binds to Fas (CD95), a receptor found on many cell types, leading to the activation of caspases and subsequent apoptosis of the target cell (often a cell infected with intracellular bacteria, such as Listeria species or a virally infected cell). The second method that activated CD8+ T cells can use for their killing function involves the release of granzymes and perforin (two compounds that bypass cell walls and active caspases). Activated CD8+ T cells also secrete IFN-? (a cytokine used in the activation of macrophages/other immune processes). [5] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Active Immunity -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Activated helper T cells begin as TH0 cells and enhance immunity by releasing cytokines such as IL-2, IFN-gamma, and IL-4 to stimulate lymphocyte growth and activate more dendritic cells. If IL-12 is present (released from dendritic cells and macrophages), the TH0 cell will transform into a TH1 cell and releases IL-2, IFN-gamma, and TNF-beta, which play roles in both the cellular and antibody responses. The production of TH1 cells becomes perpetuated by the release of IFN-gamma (also known as macrophage activation factor), which stimulates the further synthesis of IL-12. TH1 cells activate macrophages, natural killer cells, and CD8 cytotoxic T cells, which are important for intracellular infections such as viral illnesses. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Herpes Simplex Keratitis -- Pathophysiology -- Cytotoxic T cells
retrieved_docs.content: The investigation into the roles of various T cell subsets, including cytotoxic T cells (CD8 + T cells), in the context of herpes stromal keratitis, has expanded significantly over recent years, enhancing our comprehension of their contributions to the disease's pathogenesis and protection mechanisms. CD8 + T cells, known for their cytotoxic capabilities, are instrumental in the immune system's defense against viral infections. They achieve this by detecting and eliminating infected cells, facilitated by recognizing viral peptides presented by class I major histocompatibility complex (MHC) molecules. [38]

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatitis A Vaccine -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The administration of the hepatitis A vaccine causes an immune reaction by activation of white blood cells (lymphocytes) that fight infection. Immune cells engulf the vaccine antigen and release inflammatory mediators to signal the B and T cells to activate and proliferate to attack the antigen. The two types of lymphocytes, B cells, and T cells, are triggered to differentiate into new cells with specific activity against hepatitis A antigen (e.g., antibody-producing B cells, memory cells, cytotoxic and helper T cells) to give a prolonged immunity if exposed to the actual pathologic viral antigen. [1] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Respiratory Syncytial Virus Prefusion F (RSVPreF3) Vaccine -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The RSVPreF3 and RSVPreF vaccines also activate the cellular immune response by stimulating polyfunctional CD4 T cells and memory B cells. These memory cells retain a "memory" of the RSV antigens encountered during vaccination, enabling a rapid and robust immune response upon subsequent exposure to RSV. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Vaccine (Vaccination) (Archived) -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Using adjuvants enhances antibody synthesis and T-cell responses. [4] Certain compounds, including aluminum salts added to immunogens, stimulate immune responses. This effect can be mediated by two essential functions: cytokine induction that regulate T and B cell functions and increased antigen presentation in sites where lymphocytes can concentrate. Many bacterial substances can activate pattern recognition receptors [5] that activate cytokine production by antigen-presenting cells.

retrieved_docs.title: Immunotherapy -- Mechanism of Action -- Several Immunotherapy Modalities Used in Cancer Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: T-Cell Activation: Upon binding to the target antigen, the CAR activates the T-cell, leading to its proliferation, cytokine release, and cytotoxic activity against the cancer cell.

retrieved_docs.title: Posttransplantation Cancer -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Viral Infection and oncogenesis: Transplant patients are vulnerable to viral infection or reactivation of latent infection. EBV promotes the oncogenesis by reduced lymphocyte regulation, a lack of control of the oncogenic virus by EBV-specific CD81 cytotoxic T-cells, and proliferation of EBV-infected B cells. The mechanism by which HHV-8 induces oncogenesis has not been completely elucidated. HHV-8’s proinflammatory proteins might directly inhibit apoptosis and promote cell transformation. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Active Immunity -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Active immunity utilizes cytotoxic T lymphocytes, TH1, TH2 cells, and activated B cells to target infections from multiple angles. CTLs destroy infected cells, and the immunoglobulins produced by B cells target antigens in the bloodstream to bind and prevent them from reaching their target cells.

retrieved_docs.title: Herpes Simplex Keratitis -- Pathophysiology -- Cytotoxic T cells
retrieved_docs.content: Recent research underscores the potential role of CD8 + T cells in managing recurrent herpes infections. Studies have illustrated that after initial infection, HSV-1-specific CD8 + T cells are activated and migrate to the trigeminal ganglia, forming associations with neurons and playing a crucial role in the immunological memory against the virus. While some researchers assert the protective role of CD8 + T cells during HSV-1 reactivation, others propose a bystander role, indicating a nuanced understanding of these cells in the disease's pathology. [38] Significant effort has been devoted to delineating the CD8 + T cell receptor repertoire in response to herpes keratitis. Initial findings pointed towards a response predominantly targeting a single epitope on the virus's glycoprotein B (gB). Subsequent research adjusted this view, showing that the immunodominant gB epitope represents about half of the CD8 + T cells response in the trigeminal ganglia, with an array of subdominant epitopes also playing critical roles. This comprehensive understanding of T cell epitope specificity is pivotal for developing targeted HSV-1 vaccines. [41]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Cytotoxic T Cells -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The inability to eliminate pathogens in chronic infection is described as an exhaustion of T-cells. This cytotoxic function loss is caused by ongoing exposure to an antigen, which induces the expression of inhibitory receptors on CD8 T-cells. These inhibitory receptors impair T-cell receptor signaling pathways, blocking receptor activation and propagation. [7] This is the case in chronic hepatitis C infection. The persistence of the hepatitis C virus causes constant stimulation of CD8 T-cells, eventually leading to the expression of inhibitory receptors and ineffectiveness of the cytotoxic cells, and additional therapy is required to reactivate these cytotoxic cells. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1531
retrieved_docs.content: Viral antigens constitute the active ingredient of viral vaccines, providing the essential immunogenic component that stimulates the body’s immune response and confers protection against the targeted viral infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2810
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors, direct acting antivirals constitute a class of antiviral medications that includes ritonavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir. These agents function by inhibiting viral protease enzymes, thereby preventing the maturation of viral particles and serving as direct-acting antiviral therapies, particularly in the management of HIV infection. Their classification together reflects shared mechanisms of action and therapeutic use within antiviral treatment strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 281
retrieved_docs.content: Rimantadine is classified as a cyclic amine and a direct‑acting antiviral, placing it within a group of compounds that target viral replication mechanisms. A notable physiologic effect of rimantadine is the alteration of cellular synthetic activity, which can influence both viral production and host cell metabolic processes. Recognizing rimantadine’s membership in the cyclic amine, direct‑acting antiviral class and its capacity to modify cellular synthesis helps clinicians understand its mechanism of action, anticipate potential cellular effects, and optimize its use in antiviral therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 0 -- Community 21
retrieved_docs.content: these entities and relationships delineate a comprehensive framework of antiviral, antiretroviral, and antifungal drug classes, their structural families, mechanisms of action, therapeutic indications, epidemiologic links, and relevant contraindications, thereby informing optimal clinical selection, prevention, and treatment strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 0 -- Community 6
retrieved_docs.content: these entities demonstrate how diverse chemical classes—DAAs, NRTIs, other nucleoside/nucleotide analogues, integrase inhibitors, NNRTIs, cyclic amines, lipopeptide antivirals, echinocandin antifungals, peptide‑derived radiopharmaceuticals, fusion‑inhibitor peptides, monoclonal antibodies, broad‑spectrum antivirals, and acid‑suppressive agents—are organized by mechanism of action, structural relationships, disease indications, and safety considerations, thereby guiding clinicians in prevention, diagnosis, and treatment of viral infections such as HCV, HIV, cytomegalovirus, orthopoxvirus, and influenza, as well as in related therapeutic contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1231
retrieved_docs.content: Abacavir, an antiretroviral nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor used in HIV treatment, is mapped to dideoxyadenosine, indicating a direct chemical or functional correspondence between the two nucleoside analogues. This mapping highlights the structural similarity of abacavir to dideoxyadenosine, which can be relevant for understanding its mechanism of action, potential cross‑reactivity, and the design of related therapeutic agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 653
retrieved_docs.content: Cyclic amines, direct acting antivirals and neuraminidase inhibitors, direct acting antivirals are both subclasses of direct acting antivirals. Rimantadine is a member of the cyclic amine subclass and produces the physiologic effect known as Cellular Synthetic Activity Alteration, indicating that it alters cellular synthetic activity. Zanamivir is a member of the neuraminidase inhibitor subclass, placing it within the broader category of direct acting antivirals. These connections show how individual antiviral agents such as rimantadine and zanamivir are organized within specific antiviral classes and highlight rimantadine’s mechanism of altering cellular synthetic processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1991
retrieved_docs.content: The mechanism of action for merkaptamin is currently described as an unknown cellular or molecular interaction, indicating that the precise cellular or molecular processes by which merkaptamin exerts its effects have not yet been elucidated.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1314
retrieved_docs.content: toxins, are employed to safely stimulate protective immune responses, and xenon, an inert gas with anesthetic properties, influences neuronal activity and cerebral physiology. Collectively, these diverse entities serve as important qualifiers for describing and investigating physiological mechanisms, disease processes, and therapeutic interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1484
retrieved_docs.content: Ganciclovir is an antiviral agent whose mechanism of action is classified as a nucleic acid synthesis inhibitor, meaning it interferes with the synthesis of viral DNA and thereby suppresses viral replication. This pharmacologic property places ganciclovir within the broader category of nucleic acid synthesis inhibitors, highlighting its role in targeting viral replication processes and informing its use in the treatment of infections such as cytomegalovirus.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 1096
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors, classified as direct‑acting antivirals, encompass several specific agents including ritonavir, fosamprenavir, amprenavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir. All of these drugs share the common mechanism of inhibiting viral proteases, a broader relationship described as “inhibitory proteas.” Within this class, amprenavir is explicitly linked to the concept of inhibitory proteas, underscoring its role in protease inhibition. The collective activity of these protease inhibitors as direct‑acting antivirals is central to antiviral treatment strategies, particularly in the management of HIV infection, where blocking viral protease function is essential for suppressing viral replication.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2080
retrieved_docs.content: Cidofovir is classified as a member of the pharmacologic group “nucleosides and nucleotides excluding reverse transcriptase inhibitors,” indicating that it belongs to the broader category of nucleoside and nucleotide analogues but does not function as a reverse transcriptase inhibitor. This classification helps differentiate cidofovir’s mechanism of action and therapeutic use from other antiviral agents that target reverse transcriptase.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2491
retrieved_docs.content: Fosamprenavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir are all classified as members of the protease inhibitor group of direct‑acting antivirals. As protease inhibitors, each drug targets viral protease enzymes that are essential for processing viral polyproteins, thereby interrupting the replication cycle of viruses such as HIV. Their shared categorization under the “protease inhibitors, direct acting antivirals” class highlights a common mechanism of action and therapeutic use in antiviral regimens, emphasizing the importance of this drug class in managing viral infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 0 -- Community 20
retrieved_docs.content: therapies. Clinically, gemifloxacin may treat granulomatous pneumonia, a pulmonary complication that can follow influenza infection; zanamivir provides prophylaxis against influenza. Smallpox, an orthopoxvirus disease, can be treated with brincidofovir or tecovirimat. Together, these classifications, molecular targets, mechanisms of action, contraindications, and formulation details constitute an integrated framework for antiviral therapy, anticoagulation management, antifungal treatment, and peptide‑based diagnostic imaging in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 1129
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors, classified as direct‑acting antivirals, constitute a therapeutic drug class that includes ritonavir, fosamprenavir, indinavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir. These agents act by specifically inhibiting viral protease enzymes, thereby preventing the maturation of viral particles and reducing viral replication. As key components of antiretroviral therapy for HIV infection, each of these drugs—ritonavir, fosamprenavir, indinavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir—shares the common mechanism of direct protease inhibition, making them integral to combination regimens aimed at achieving viral suppression and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 512
retrieved_docs.content: VIRUSES AND FUNGI) are organized into subgroups (aminoglykosidy, glycopeptide antibacterials) that connect specific drugs (telavancin, odiparcil, daptomycin, micronomicin) through shared structural features (glycosides) and common mechanisms of action (protein‑synthesis inhibition). This integrated view aids clinicians in understanding the relationships among antimicrobial agents, their classifications, and their therapeutic targets.
